events.text: The patient is a 71-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is of Polish-American descent, retired steelworker, with a significant smoking history (45 pack-years, quit age 62). He has a family history notable for a brother with pancreatic cancer at age 58 and a father with prostate cancer at age 74. He has known comorbidities including type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin), hypertension (on lisinopril and amlodipine), and mild chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR ~52). His ECOG performance status at time of initial cancer diagnosis was 1.

At age 67 years, the patient was diagnosed with adenocarcinoma of the prostate, AJCC stage IVB (M1b), with bilateral pelvic lymph node involvement and multiple bone metastases at presentation. Primary site: prostate (ICD-O-3 site code C61.9). Histology: acinar adenocarcinoma (ICD-O-3 histology code 8140/3). Gleason score: 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5. PSA at diagnosis: 187 ng/mL. Clinical T-stage: cT3b (bilateral seminal vesicle invasion on MRI). Regional lymph nodes: N1 (bilateral obturator and external iliac nodes, largest 1.8 cm). Distant metastases: M1b (bone: L2 vertebral body, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, multiple rib lesions). Bone scan showed 8 discrete lesions. No visceral metastases at diagnosis. Confirmation: positive biopsy (12-core TRUS-guided biopsy, all 12 cores positive). Tumor size: not applicable (diffuse involvement). Laterality: bilateral. Perineural invasion: present. Lymphovascular invasion: present.

At age 67 years, the patient had a transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy. Gross description: 12 core biopsy specimens, each 1.2-1.5 cm in length. Microscopic findings: acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason pattern 5+4=9, Grade Group 5. All 12 cores involved, with 60-95% core involvement. Perineural invasion identified in 8 of 12 cores. Extensive intraductal carcinoma component noted. No small cell or neuroendocrine differentiation identified. Immunohistochemistry: PSA positive, PSMA positive, AR positive (strong, diffuse), Ki-67 approximately 35%. ERG by IHC: negative. PTEN by IHC: loss of expression (heterogeneous). Synaptophysin and chromogranin: negative.

At age 67 years, the patient had a Technetium-99m bone scan and CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis for staging. Bone scan findings: 8 discrete foci of increased radiotracer uptake consistent with osseous metastatic disease, involving: L2 vertebral body, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs, T9 vertebral body, and right femoral neck. No superscan pattern. CT findings: prostate gland enlarged (estimated volume 48 cc) with extraprostatic extension and bilateral seminal vesicle invasion. Bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy: right obturator node 1.8 cm, left external iliac node 1.4 cm, right external iliac node 1.2 cm. Retroperitoneal nodes: common iliac nodes up to 1.1 cm, below threshold. No hepatic, pulmonary, or adrenal lesions. Cancer status: metastatic disease present.

At age 67 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed on the diagnostic prostate biopsy specimen. Germline testing also performed from peripheral blood. Tumor NGS (FoundationOne CDx): BRCA2 p.E1308* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, VAF 48%), TP53 p.R248Q (pathogenic, VAF 52%), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, exons 1-9), RB1 loss (focal deletion), CDK12 p.L786fs*2 (frameshift, VAF 31%), FOXA1 p.P358L (VAF 28%), SPOP p.F133V (VAF 14%). No AR amplification. No AR ligand-binding domain mutations detected. TMB: 4.2 mutations/Mb (low). MSI status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Copy number alterations: BRCA2 loss, PTEN homozygous deletion, MYC gain (8q24), AR locus: not amplified. No TMPRSS2-ERG fusion detected. CDK4/CDK6: no amplification. No FGFR alterations. No PIK3CA mutations. BRCA2 germline confirmatory testing from blood: BRCA2 p.E1308* pathogenic germline variant confirmed (heterozygous in germline, LOH in tumor). Germline genetic counseling referral placed.

At age 67 years, the patient was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg intramuscular every 3 months (LHRH agonist) plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily, as first-line systemic therapy for metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (mCSPC). Treatment intent: palliative/disease control. Baseline testosterone: 412 ng/dL. Target: castrate levels (<50 ng/dL). Docetaxel was discussed but deferred given patient preference and CKD.

At age 67 years (3 months after initiating ADT + abiraterone), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. PSA had declined from 187 ng/mL to 4.2 ng/mL (97.8% decline). Testosterone: 18 ng/dL (castrate). Patient reporting fatigue grade 1, hot flashes grade 2, and mild lower extremity edema grade 1 (attributed to abiraterone/prednisone). No new pain complaints. ECOG PS: 1. Physical exam: no palpable lymphadenopathy. Continues on metformin (held briefly perioperatively), lisinopril dose reduced due to blood pressure well controlled. Labs: ALP 98 U/L (normalized from 187 at baseline), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (mild decline from 13.2 baseline). Cancer status: disease responding.

At age 67.5 years, the patient had a restaging bone scan and CT of the abdomen/pelvis. Bone scan findings: significant improvement from prior study. Previously noted lesions at left iliac wing, right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs show markedly decreased uptake, consistent with treatment response. L2, T9, right acetabulum and right femoral neck lesions persist but with decreased intensity. Overall: partial response in osseous disease. CT findings: pelvic lymph nodes have regressed; previously noted right obturator node now 0.8 cm, left external iliac node 0.6 cm. Prostate stable in size. No new visceral lesions. Cancer status: responding, residual metastatic disease present.

At age 69 years, the patient had an oncology visit for rising PSA. PSA now 8.7 ng/mL (nadir had been 1.1 ng/mL approximately 14 months prior). Testosterone: 22 ng/dL (confirmed castrate). Two consecutive rising PSA values over 3 months confirm biochemical progression consistent with castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC). Patient reports new back pain (grade 2, lumbar region) over the past 6 weeks. Bone pain score: 3/10. Performance status ECOG 1. No new neurological symptoms. No urinary obstruction. Abiraterone discontinued. Plan: cross-sectional imaging and bone scan for restaging, then initiation of next-line systemic therapy. Hemoglobin: 10.9 g/dL.

At age 69 years, the patient had a restaging CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan following biochemical CRPC progression. Bone scan: progressive osseous disease. New lesions identified in right femoral neck (increased), new lesion in left femoral head, new lesion in T12 vertebral body. Previously responding lesions in left iliac wing and right acetabulum now show increased uptake. Total: 12 lesions (increase from 8). CT findings: right obturator lymph node now 1.6 cm (previously 0.8 cm, progression). New left common iliac node 1.2 cm. No hepatic lesions. No pulmonary nodules. No adrenal lesions. Prostate/seminal vesicle region: stable post-treatment changes. Cancer status: progressive disease, bone and lymph node metastases.

At age 69 years, following confirmed CRPC with disease progression on ADT + abiraterone, the patient was initiated on enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily (4 x 40 mg capsules) as second-line hormonal therapy for mCRPC. ADT (leuprolide) continued to maintain castrate testosterone. Baseline PSA: 8.7 ng/mL. Baseline hemoglobin: 10.9 g/dL. Treating oncologist noted BRCA2 germline mutation and discussed future olaparib eligibility. Patient enrolled in institutional data registry.

At age 69 years (2 months after starting enzalutamide), the patient developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 cognitive slowing (subjective word-finding difficulty reported by patient and confirmed by family). Blood pressure mildly elevated to 152/94 mmHg. Antihypertensive medications adjusted (amlodipine increased to 10 mg daily, lisinopril increased to 20 mg daily). Fatigue attributed to enzalutamide; managed conservatively with activity modification and sleep hygiene counseling. No falls. No seizures. CTCAE grade: fatigue grade 2, cognitive disorder grade 1. No dose reduction required.

At age 69.5 years (6 months after starting enzalutamide), the patient had oncology follow-up showing PSA response. PSA: 1.4 ng/mL (decline of 83.9% from baseline of 8.7 ng/mL). Testosterone: 19 ng/dL. Patient reports ongoing grade 1-2 fatigue but improved from peak. Back pain improved, now 1/10 with stable analgesic requirements (acetaminophen 1g TID as needed). ECOG PS: 1. Labs: hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL (stable mild anemia, attributed to chronic disease and ADT). ALP: 112 U/L. LDH: 186 U/L. eGFR stable at 48. Assessment: good PSA response to enzalutamide, continue current therapy. Bone-directed therapy: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks initiated for skeletal metastases (dose-adjusted for CKD). Cancer status: responding to enzalutamide.

At age 69.5 years, the patient had a restaging bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis on enzalutamide. Bone scan findings: overall improvement in osseous disease. Previously progressive lesions show decreased uptake. The T12 and left femoral head lesions show partial response. Right femoral neck lesion stable. No new osseous lesions. CT findings: regression of pelvic lymphadenopathy; right obturator node now 0.9 cm, left common iliac node 0.8 cm. No new visceral lesions. Cancer status: partial response, residual osseous and nodal metastases.

At age 70.2 years, the patient had restaging imaging after 9 months on enzalutamide, prompted by two rising PSA values. Bone scan: new lesions identified. New foci of increased uptake in left iliac crest (2 lesions), right proximal humerus, and sacrum. Previously stable right femoral neck lesion shows increased uptake. Total osseous lesions: approximately 17 (significantly increased from 12 at CRPC diagnosis). CT abdomen/pelvis: enlargement of right obturator node to 2.1 cm. New retroperitoneal nodes: right para-aortic 1.4 cm, aortocaval 1.1 cm. No hepatic or pulmonary metastases at this time. PSA at time of imaging: 14.3 ng/mL. Cancer status: progressive disease with new bone metastases and nodal progression.

At age 70.2 years, the patient had an oncology visit confirming enzalutamide resistance with progressive disease. PSA: 14.3 ng/mL (nadir 1.4 ng/mL, now rising x3 values over 4 months). Testosterone: 21 ng/dL. New bone pain in left hip and shoulder (grade 2-3). Performance status: ECOG 2 (fatigue limiting activity beyond 50% of waking hours). Hemoglobin: 9.6 g/dL. Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (mild worsening). Platelets: 194,000. ANC: 3.2 K/uL. Discussion: given germline BRCA2 mutation, patient is a strong candidate for olaparib (PARP inhibitor), supported by PROfound trial data. Enzalutamide discontinued. Plan: initiate olaparib. Continued ADT. Bone pain managed with extended-release oxycodone 10 mg BID added, zoledronic acid continued. Referred to radiation oncology for consideration of palliative radiation to symptomatic right femoral neck lesion. Cancer status: progressing on enzalutamide.

At age 70.3 years, the patient received palliative radiation therapy to the right femoral neck and proximal femur for pain control and fracture prevention. Technique: 3D conformal external beam radiation therapy (EBRT). Dose: 30 Gy in 10 fractions (3 Gy/fraction). Field: right proximal femur including femoral neck and lesser trochanter. Treatment duration: 2 weeks. Tolerance: good. Acute side effects: grade 1 skin erythema at treatment site, grade 1 nausea. Pain response: significant improvement noted by end of treatment, pain score reduced from 7/10 to 2/10 at treated site. Orthopedic surgery consulted: prophylactic intramedullary nailing not required at this time given cortical integrity maintained on CT.

At age 70.3 years, the patient was initiated on olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily (tablets) for mCRPC with BRCA2 germline mutation, following progression on enzalutamide. ADT (leuprolide acetate) continued. Olaparib indicated based on BRCA2 alteration per PROfound trial data. Pre-treatment labs: hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL, platelets 194,000/uL, ANC 3.2 K/uL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR ~42). No dose reduction required at initiation (eGFR threshold for dose reduction is <31 mL/min). Baseline PSA: 14.3 ng/mL. Patient counseled on risk of myelosuppression, particularly anemia, nausea, and fatigue. MDS/AML risk discussed.

At age 70.5 years (2 months after starting olaparib), the patient had oncology follow-up. PSA: 4.8 ng/mL (66% decline from 14.3 ng/mL baseline). Testosterone: 18 ng/dL. Patient reports improved pain overall, right hip pain now 1/10. New complaints: worsening fatigue (grade 2-3), nausea (grade 1-2, managed with ondansetron PRN), and increasing dyspnea on exertion. Labs: hemoglobin 7.9 g/dL (decrease from 9.6 g/dL, attributed to olaparib-associated anemia). Reticulocyte count: 1.1% (hypoproliferative pattern). MCV: 96 fL (normocytic). Iron studies: ferritin 312, TIBC normal, transferrin saturation 28% - pattern consistent with anemia of chronic disease/bone marrow suppression rather than iron deficiency. Platelets: 168,000. ANC: 2.6 K/uL. Assessment: significant PSA response to olaparib; olaparib-related anemia requiring monitoring. Olaparib continued at current dose. Iron supplementation initiated (ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily). Cancer status: responding to olaparib.

At age 70.7 years (4 months after starting olaparib), the patient presented to the infusion center for transfusion support. Hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL with symptomatic anemia: dyspnea at rest, orthostatic hypotension (systolic BP drop 20 mmHg standing), heart rate 102 bpm, fatigue grade 3. CTCAE grade 3 anemia. Patient received 2 units of packed red blood cells (pRBCs). Post-transfusion hemoglobin: 9.1 g/dL. Symptoms improved. Olaparib dose reduced to 200 mg twice daily per protocol for grade 3 hematologic toxicity. Hematology consulted; bone marrow suppression most likely etiology. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) considered but not initiated given active malignancy concerns. Bone marrow biopsy recommended but deferred per patient preference.

At age 71 years (7 months after starting olaparib), the patient again developed symptomatic anemia requiring transfusion. Hemoglobin: 6.5 g/dL. Symptoms: significant fatigue, exertional dyspnea, palpitations. Received 2 units pRBCs; post-transfusion hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL. Additionally noted: platelet count 98,000/uL (grade 2 thrombocytopenia) and ANC 1.4 K/uL (grade 2 neutropenia). Pattern consistent with cumulative bone marrow suppression from olaparib. Olaparib held for 1 week, then restarted at 100 mg twice daily (second dose reduction). PSA at this visit: 6.2 ng/mL (still below baseline of 14.3 ng/mL despite dose reduction). Consideration of treatment discontinuation discussed but patient opted to continue given partial disease control. CTCAE: anemia grade 3, thrombocytopenia grade 2, neutropenia grade 2.

At age 71 years (7 months on olaparib, reduced dose), the patient had restaging CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan. Bone scan findings: overall stable osseous disease compared to prior study at time of enzalutamide progression. Previously progressive lesions show partial response. No definitively new bone lesions. CT findings: pelvic and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy stable to slightly improved. Right obturator node: 1.6 cm (previously 2.1 cm). Para-aortic nodes stable. NEW FINDING: Liver - 2 hypodense lesions identified in right hepatic lobe, measuring 1.1 cm and 0.9 cm. Lungs: 2 small pulmonary nodules in right lower lobe (5 mm and 4 mm, indeterminate but new). No pleural effusion. Concerning for early visceral metastatic disease. Cancer status: partial response in osseous/nodal disease but new possible visceral metastases requiring further evaluation.

At age 71 years, the patient had a gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the liver and a PET/CT (F-18 FDG) for further characterization of hepatic lesions seen on CT. MRI liver: 2 lesions in segment VI-VII of right hepatic lobe measuring 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm. Lesions demonstrate arterial phase enhancement with washout on delayed phase. Appearance most consistent with hepatic metastases. No biliary dilation. PET/CT: hypermetabolic activity in right hepatic lobe corresponding to MRI lesions (SUVmax 5.8 and 4.2). Extensive hypermetabolic osseous disease (multiple sites as previously noted). Bilateral lung nodules show low-level FDG avidity (right lower lobe 5 mm, SUVmax 1.8 - indeterminate). Pelvic and retroperitoneal nodes: stable, mildly FDG avid. Impression: new hepatic metastases confirmed, indeterminate pulmonary nodules with low FDG avidity, stable bone and nodal disease. Cancer status: progression with visceral (hepatic) metastases.

At age 71 years, the patient underwent CT-guided liver biopsy of the dominant right hepatic lobe lesion (1.2 cm lesion in segment VI-VII). Gross description: 2 core biopsy specimens, each approximately 1.5 cm. Microscopic findings: metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with prostatic origin. Glands are poorly formed with high-grade cytologic atypia, consistent with Gleason pattern 5. Immunohistochemistry: PSA positive (focal, weak), PSMA positive (moderate, diffuse), NKX3.1 positive (strong, diffuse), AR positive (moderate), CK7 negative, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, TTF-1 negative. Synaptophysin: focally positive (1+ in <5% cells). Chromogranin: negative. Ki-67: approximately 60% (markedly increased from primary biopsy). Note: focal synaptophysin positivity with high Ki-67 raises concern for early neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer, t-NEPC), though morphology predominantly adenocarcinoma at this time.

At age 71 years, next-generation sequencing was performed on the liver metastasis biopsy specimen (FoundationOne CDx) to assess resistance mechanisms. Results: BRCA2 p.E1308* (pathogenic, VAF 61%, consistent with retained driver), TP53 p.R248Q (VAF 58%), PTEN loss (retained homozygous deletion), RB1 loss (retained), AR amplification (NEW: copy number 12, high-level amplification), AR p.L702H (NEW: ligand-binding domain mutation, associated with abiraterone resistance and possible enzalutamide cross-resistance), CDK12 p.L786fs*2 (retained, VAF 44%), MYC amplification (8q24, increased CN from prior), BRCA2 reversion mutation: NONE detected (no secondary BRCA2 mutations that would restore open reading frame). New alterations not present in primary: PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 22%), AURKA amplification (NEW). No olaparib resistance reversion mutations in BRCA2. BRCA1: wild-type. ATM: wild-type. MSI: microsatellite stable. TMB: 6.1 mutations/Mb (low-intermediate). Relevant fusions screened: no TMPRSS2-ERG, no NTRK, no ALK, no RET fusions detected. NEPC markers by NGS: RB1 loss and TP53 mutation present (supports vulnerability to neuroendocrine transdifferentiation).

At age 71 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and subsequent oncology visit regarding disease progression with visceral (hepatic) metastases. Summary: mCRPC with BRCA2 germline mutation, now with hepatic metastases and pathologic evidence of possible early neuroendocrine transdifferentiation. AR amplification and AR LBD mutation identified on liquid/tissue biopsy. Olaparib at reduced dose (100 mg BID) with ongoing anemia requiring recurrent transfusions. PSA now 11.8 ng/mL (rising). Performance status: ECOG 2. Weight loss: 8 lbs over prior 3 months. Albumin: 3.1 g/dL. Hemoglobin: 7.2 g/dL (pre-transfusion). Bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL. ALP: 287 U/L (rising, new hepatic involvement). LDH: 412 U/L (elevated, disease burden). Creatinine: 1.7 mg/dL. Options discussed: (1) platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin + cabazitaxel, given BRCA2 and neuroendocrine features) vs. (2) cabazitaxel monotherapy vs. (3) lutetium-177 PSMA (if PSMA-avid; PET/CT showed PSMA expression on prior PSMA PET data), vs. (4) clinical trial. Patient requests treatment with disease control intent but prioritizes quality of life. Decision: transition to carboplatin + cabazitaxel given NEPC features and BRCA2. Hospice discussed as future option. Cancer status: progressing with visceral metastases.

At age 71 years (following confirmed visceral progression on reduced-dose olaparib), olaparib was discontinued. The patient was initiated on carboplatin AUC 4 IV day 1 + cabazitaxel 20 mg/m2 IV day 1 every 21 days for mCRPC with neuroendocrine features and BRCA2 mutation (supported by retrospective data and institutional protocol). Prophylactic G-CSF (filgrastim) added given prior cytopenias and age. ADT (leuprolide) continued. Dexamethasone 8 mg premedication used for cabazitaxel. Baseline: PSA 11.8 ng/mL, hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL (post-transfusion), ANC 1.6 K/uL, platelets 102,000. Baseline ECG: normal sinus rhythm. Treatment intent: palliative disease control.

At age 71.2 years (after cycle 1 of carboplatin + cabazitaxel), the patient developed febrile neutropenia (temperature 38.9 degrees C, ANC 0.3 K/uL, grade 4 neutropenia). Hospitalized for 5 days for IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam, then deescalated to oral ciprofloxacin). Blood cultures: no growth. Chest X-ray: no pneumonia. The patient also had grade 3 anemia (hemoglobin 6.3 g/dL) requiring 2 units pRBCs and grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 78,000). Peripheral neuropathy grade 1 (sensory, bilateral fingertips). Nausea grade 2 managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine. Hospitalization complicated by transient AKI (creatinine peak 2.1 mg/dL, returned to 1.8 mg/dL). CTCAE: neutropenia grade 4, anemia grade 3, thrombocytopenia grade 2, AKI grade 2, peripheral neuropathy grade 1. Cabazitaxel dose reduced to 15 mg/m2 for future cycles; carboplatin AUC reduced to 3.

At age 71.4 years, the patient had restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis after 2 cycles of carboplatin + cabazitaxel. CT findings: liver - previously noted lesions in right hepatic lobe are now 1.4 cm and 1.3 cm (slight increase in size from 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm, indicating disease progression despite chemotherapy). New hepatic lesion: 0.6 cm in segment V (new). Lung: previously indeterminate right lower lobe nodule now 9 mm (increased from 5 mm), second nodule 6 mm (increased from 4 mm), raising concern for pulmonary metastases. Pelvic lymphadenopathy: right obturator node now 2.4 cm (increased). Bones: not adequately evaluated on CT, clinical symptoms suggest stable-to-progressive osseous disease. PSA at time of imaging: 18.6 ng/mL (rising). Assessment: progressive disease, hepatic and pulmonary metastases, nodal progression. Cancer status: progressive visceral disease, treatment-refractory mCRPC.

At age 71.4 years, the patient had an oncology visit after confirmation of disease progression on carboplatin + cabazitaxel. PSA: 18.6 ng/mL. Performance status: ECOG 3 (in bed or chair more than 50% of waking hours). Weight loss: 15 lbs total over 5 months. Hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL (transfusion-dependent, 3rd transfusion in 2 months). ALP: 418 U/L. LDH: 689 U/L. Bilirubin: 1.8 mg/dL (rising, hepatic compromise). Albumin: 2.7 g/dL. eGFR: 36 mL/min. Patient has significant fatigue, anorexia, and functional decline. Bone pain 5/10 overall. Pain service consulted; opioid regimen escalated to oxycodone ER 20 mg BID with oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN, with plan to transition to transdermal fentanyl. Further chemotherapy: not advisable given ECOG 3, hepatic and renal compromise, and prior toxicity. Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 discussed but hepatic function and performance status are relative contraindications at this time. Clinical trial options reviewed: no available trials for which patient is eligible. Palliative care and hospice services engaged in depth. Patient and family meeting held. Goals of care: comfort-focused, prioritizing quality of life. Carboplatin + cabazitaxel discontinued. ADT continued for symptom control (testosterone flare prevention). Cancer status: progressive, treatment-refractory mCRPC with visceral metastases.

At age 71.6 years, the patient transitioned to hospice care. Enrolled with a home hospice agency. Symptoms at time of hospice enrollment: severe fatigue (grade 4), moderate-severe bone pain managed with transdermal fentanyl 25 mcg/hour with oral breakthrough dosing, decreased appetite and oral intake, progressive dyspnea (grade 2, likely multifactorial: anemia, hepatic disease, possible pulmonary metastases). Hemoglobin at last check: 6.9 g/dL; transfusions ongoing at monthly intervals for symptom palliation per patient preference. PSA last measured: 34.2 ng/mL. Performance status: ECOG 4 (predominantly bedbound). Patient is alert and communicative with family. Advanced directive in place: DNR/DNI. Durable power of attorney: wife designated. Cancer status: end-stage metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with hepatic, pulmonary, and extensive osseous metastases. Disease trajectory: terminal.

documents.text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]
PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for oncology follow-up; assessment of response to enzalutamide in the setting of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Approximately 6 months since enzalutamide initiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) who presents today for routine follow-up approximately 6 months after initiating enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg orally once daily. He was originally diagnosed with stage IVB (M1b) acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate approximately 2.5 years ago at age 67, with Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5 disease. Initial PSA was 187 ng/mL. He was found at diagnosis to have bilateral pelvic lymph node involvement and 8 discrete bone metastases (L2, T9, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, right femoral neck, right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs). Germline testing at diagnosis confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 p.E1308* variant. PTEN loss on IHC and somatic RB1 deletion were also noted.

He was treated with first-line ADT (leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM Q3 months) plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID for metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (mCSPC), achieving an excellent PSA nadir of 1.1 ng/mL. He had a sustained response for approximately 14 months before developing biochemical progression, confirmed by two consecutive rising PSA values. Restaging imaging at the time of CRPC transition demonstrated progression with new bone lesions (total 12), enlarging pelvic lymph nodes, and new retroperitoneal nodes. He was transitioned to enzalutamide 160 mg daily (abiraterone discontinued, ADT continued) at that time.

Two months into enzalutamide therapy, he developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 subjective cognitive slowing, with mild hypertension that required uptitration of his antihypertensives (amlodipine increased to 10 mg, lisinopril increased to 20 mg daily). No seizure activity. No falls. These side effects were managed conservatively and improved over subsequent months.

Today, at approximately 6 months on enzalutamide, Mr. Kowalski reports the following:
- Back pain: substantially improved. He previously rated lumbar pain at 3/10 at the time of CRPC diagnosis; currently 1/10, well-controlled on acetaminophen 1g TID as needed (no opioids required).
- Fatigue: ongoing grade 1-2, improved from peak grade 2-3 seen at the 2-month mark. He describes it as manageable and has been able to perform household activities, light yard work, and attend family events.
- Hot flashes: ongoing, grade 1-2, attributed to ongoing ADT. He uses a fan at night and has adapted his sleep routine.
- Cognitive symptoms: mild subjective word-finding difficulty persists at grade 1. Family confirms no significant functional impairment. He continues to manage his own finances and medications.
- No new bone pain, no neurological symptoms, no lower extremity weakness or bowel/bladder dysfunction to suggest cord compromise.
- Appetite: good. No significant weight change. Diet well-maintained.
- No dyspnea, no chest pain, no hemoptysis.
- No lower urinary tract symptoms. No hematuria.
- Bowel function: normal.
- Ambulating without assistance.

He has been compliant with enzalutamide (confirmed by pill count and patient report). He takes the 4 x 40 mg capsules together once daily in the morning with food. He denies missing more than 1-2 doses over the 6-month period.

Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV Q12 weeks was initiated at this visit or prior (bone-directed therapy), dose-adjusted for chronic kidney disease (eGFR ~48). He takes calcium and vitamin D supplementation. He was counseled on osteonecrosis of the jaw and dental hygiene; dental clearance is on file.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1-2 as noted. No fevers, chills, or night sweats. Appetite stable. No significant weight loss.
Neurological: Grade 1 cognitive slowing (subjective). No seizures. No headaches. No focal weakness or sensory deficits. No visual changes.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension, currently well-controlled on adjusted regimen. No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema (mild, grade 1, unchanged).
Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest or with exertion. No cough.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. Bowel habits regular.
Genitourinary: No urinary symptoms. No hematuria. No dysuria. Libido absent (expected given ADT).
Musculoskeletal: Mild residual low back discomfort, 1/10, stable. No new joint pain. No fracture symptoms.
Hematologic: No bruising, bleeding, or signs of infection.
Endocrine: Hot flashes, grade 1-2. No polydipsia or polyuria beyond baseline (diabetes well-controlled per patient).
Skin: No rash. No hair changes beyond expected from ADT.
Psychiatric: Mild low mood acknowledged. Denies depression, anxiety, or suicidal ideation. Supported by wife and adult children.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bone and lymph node metastases -- as described above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- on metformin (held when contrast given)
3. Hypertension -- on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily
4. Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a-3b -- baseline creatinine 1.4-1.6 mg/dL, eGFR ~48-52; monitored closely
5. Prior tobacco use: 45 pack-year smoking history; quit at age 62

SURGICAL HISTORY
- Transrectal ultrasound-guided 12-core prostate biopsy (diagnostic, at age 67)
- No prostatectomy; patient was not a surgical candidate given metastatic presentation

FAMILY HISTORY
- Brother: pancreatic cancer, age 58 (deceased)
- Father: prostate cancer, age 74 (deceased)
- No known maternal malignancies reported
- Germline BRCA2 p.E1308* pathogenic variant confirmed in patient; family genetic counseling referral placed previously

SOCIAL HISTORY
- Retired steelworker, Polish-American descent
- Married, lives with wife; adult children involved in care
- Former smoker: 45 pack-years, quit age 62
- No alcohol use currently
- No illicit drug use
- Functional at baseline -- manages ADLs independently; limited IADLs due to fatigue

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS
1. Enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg (4 x 40 mg capsules) orally once daily

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, Gleason 9 (5+4), with germline BRCA2 p.E1308* and somatic PTEN/RB1 loss, who is now approximately 6 months into enzalutamide therapy following progression on ADT + abiraterone.

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER -- RESPONDING TO ENZALUTAMIDE

He has had an excellent response to enzalutamide. PSA has declined from 8.7 ng/mL at the time of enzalutamide initiation to the current value of 1.4 ng/mL, representing an 83.9% decline. Testosterone is confirmed at castrate levels (19 ng/dL). Restaging imaging demonstrates partial response in both bone and lymph node disease with no new lesions identified. Clinically, his back pain has improved substantially and his performance status is maintained at ECOG 1.

Plan:
- Continue enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily. No dose adjustment at this time.
- Continue ADT with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM Q3 months; next injection to be scheduled.
- Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV Q12 weeks (next infusion in approximately 8 weeks). Monitor renal function and calcium closely.
- Repeat PSA and testosterone in 8-10 weeks.
- Restaging imaging (bone scan + CT) in approximately 6 months or sooner if PSA rises significantly or new symptoms arise.
- Closely monitor for enzalutamide-related adverse effects, particularly cognitive and cardiovascular effects.

2. ENZALUTAMIDE-RELATED ADVERSE EFFECTS

The patient experienced grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 cognitive slowing early in the course, managed conservatively. Fatigue has improved to grade 1-2. Cognitive symptoms remain mild (grade 1) and non-functionally limiting.

Hypertension was uptitrated (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg) and is currently reasonably controlled today at 138/82 mmHg. Will continue to monitor at each visit.

Plan:
- Fatigue: counsel on moderate aerobic exercise (walking program), sleep hygiene, and pacing. No dose reduction required.
- Cognitive symptoms: continue to monitor. Neuropsychological referral to be considered if worsens. Counseled patient and wife to report any new cognitive changes promptly, and to monitor for falls or balance issues.
- No seizure prophylaxis indicated currently.
- Falls precautions reviewed (bathroom grab bars, clear pathways at home). Patient has a dog that he is aware of as a trip hazard.

3. ANEMIA -- CHRONIC, MULTIFACTORIAL

Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL today, mildly down from 10.9 g/dL at enzalutamide initiation but stable over recent months. Likely related to ADT effects on erythropoiesis and underlying chronic disease/CKD. No acute bleeding source identified.

Plan:
- Monitor CBC with each lab check. No transfusion indicated at this time.
- Iron studies: will send at next visit to evaluate for any correctable component. Currently on no iron supplementation.
- If hemoglobin drops below 9.0 g/dL or patient becomes symptomatic, will reassess including consideration of hematology referral.

4. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE (Stage 3b, eGFR ~48)

Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, eGFR 48 mL/min. Mild worsening from baseline of 1.4 mg/dL; not acutely concerning. Continue to monitor closely, particularly given zoledronic acid use and contrast exposures. Metformin: patient is currently using metformin -- given eGFR 48, this is at the lower range of acceptable use; will continue with close monitoring but should hold if eGFR falls below 30 or if patient receives contrast.

Plan:
- BMP with each oncology visit.
- Hold metformin 48 hours before and after any IV contrast administration.
- Zoledronic acid dose: patient is receiving 4 mg Q12 weeks (reduced from standard Q4 weeks). Per institutional guidelines, the dose is appropriate given eGFR 40-60 range. Will reassess if eGFR declines further.

5. DIABETES MELLITUS TYPE 2 -- REASONABLY CONTROLLED

HbA1c 6.9% (from 4 weeks prior). Fasting glucose 118 mg/dL today. Well-controlled. Note: corticosteroid use (prednisone carried over from abiraterone era) may contribute to hyperglycemia -- confirm below whether prednisone should be discontinued.

IMPORTANT NOTE REGARDING PREDNISONE: Prednisone 5 mg BID was co-prescribed with abiraterone. Abiraterone was discontinued approximately 3 months ago when enzalutamide was initiated. It is not standard practice to continue prednisone with enzalutamide. Medication reconciliation performed today: patient confirmed he is no longer taking prednisone (was discontinued by the covering pharmacist at the time of transition -- confirmed). Medication list updated accordingly.

6. HYPERTENSION

Currently on lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 10 mg. BP today 138/82 mmHg. Reasonable control. No change needed today.

7. GERMLINE BRCA2 STATUS AND FUTURE TREATMENT PLANNING

As discussed at prior visits, the patient carries a germline pathogenic BRCA2 p.E1308* variant, with loss of heterozygosity in the tumor. This is a therapeutically actionable alteration. He was previously informed about olaparib (a PARP inhibitor, Lynparza) and its efficacy in BRCA2-mutant mCRPC per the PROfound trial data.

At this time, enzalutamide is providing good disease control. Olaparib remains a priority option upon enzalutamide progression. In the event of confirmed biochemical or radiographic progression on enzalutamide, the transition plan would be to discontinue enzalutamide and initiate olaparib 300 mg BID.

Plan:
- Continue enzalutamide as long as effective.
- Remind patient and family of olaparib as the intended next-line therapy given BRCA2 mutation.
- Genetic counseling: recommend family members (children) pursue BRCA2 testing given germline nature; referral letter provided.

Patient remains on zoledronic acid Q12 weeks. Calcium and Vitamin D supplementation ongoing. Dental hygiene counseled -- patient has maintained regular dental care and no procedures are planned.

No pathological fractures identified on imaging. Will continue current approach. Orthopedic surgery was previously consulted regarding right femoral neck lesion, which has responded to enzalutamide and does not require surgical intervention at this time.

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old man with mCRPC, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, who has developed confirmed biochemical and radiographic progression on enzalutamide. He progressed following an approximately 8-month response (PSA nadir 1.4 ng/mL) with PSA now at 14.3 ng/mL, accompanied by new osseous lesions in the left iliac crest, right proximal humerus, and sacrum, worsening right femoral neck involvement, and new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Performance status has declined to ECOG 2, driven largely by pain and fatigue. There are no visceral metastases currently. He has no evidence of spinal cord compromise.

The genomic landscape of this patient's disease, including the confirmed germline BRCA2 p.E1308* with loss of heterozygosity in the tumor (a pathogenic truncating mutation leading to homologous recombination deficiency), strongly supports use of a PARP inhibitor. The PROfound trial demonstrated a significant progression-free and overall survival benefit for olaparib over physician's choice second-line NHA in patients with mCRPC and BRCA1/2 mutations who had progressed on a prior NHA (abiraterone or enzalutamide), and olaparib has FDA approval in this setting. Mr. Kowalski has now progressed on both abiraterone (first-line mHSPC) and enzalutamide (first-line mCRPC), and is therefore an ideal candidate for olaparib (Lynparza).

Symptom management: new left hip and shoulder pain attributable to radiographically confirmed lesions in the left iliac crest region and right proximal humerus respectively. Referral to radiation oncology for palliative EBRT to the right femoral neck lesion was completed at the prior visit, and he benefited significantly from that treatment. We will reassess whether additional palliative radiation is warranted to newly symptomatic sites.

Renal function has mildly worsened (eGFR ~42 mL/min); olaparib does not require dose adjustment until eGFR falls below 31 mL/min, so standard dosing of 300 mg BID is planned. Baseline cytopenias are present (Hgb 9.6 g/dL), and the risk of olaparib-associated myelosuppression, particularly anemia, was thoroughly discussed.

PLAN:

1. ENZALUTAMIDE: Discontinue enzalutamide effective today. No taper required.

2. OLAPARIB (Lynparza): Initiate olaparib tablets 300 mg orally twice daily. Indication: mCRPC with germline BRCA2 pathogenic mutation, progressed on prior NHA (abiraterone and enzalutamide), per PROfound trial data and FDA approval. Prior authorization submitted. Patient counseled extensively on:
   - Expected benefit: PSA response expected in approximately 60-70% of BRCA2-mutant mCRPC; improvement in pain and functional status possible.
   - Toxicities: myelosuppression (anemia most common, often requiring dose reduction or transfusion support), nausea and vomiting, fatigue, pneumonitis (rare), thromboembolic events. Risk of MDS/AML with prolonged use discussed.
   - Drug interactions: avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers. No significant interactions with current regimen.
   - Will need CBC monitoring every 4 weeks for the first year, then every 3 months.

3. ADT: Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months. Maintain castrate suppression. Next scheduled injection in 6 weeks.

4. BONE-DIRECTED THERAPY: Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks (dose-adjusted for CKD, eGFR ~42). Dental exam instructed; patient confirms no recent dental procedures planned. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continued.

5. PAIN MANAGEMENT:
   - Continue extended-release oxycodone 10 mg BID. Given new pain sites (left hip, shoulder), dose may require uptitration; patient advised to contact office if pain consistently above 5/10 or if analgesic requirements increase acutely.
   - Continue scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID.
   - Referral placed to palliative care team for co-management of pain and symptom burden.
   - Radiation oncology notified of new symptomatic lesions; will evaluate for palliative EBRT to left iliac crest/hip region if pain not controlled with systemic therapy.

6. MONITORING:
   - PSA, testosterone, CBC with differential, CMP (including creatinine, LFTs) in 4 weeks.
   - Restaging bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned after approximately 3 months on olaparib, or sooner if clinically indicated.
   - Close monitoring of hemoglobin given baseline anemia and PARP inhibitor-related myelosuppression risk.

7. RENAL FUNCTION: eGFR 42 mL/min. Continue metformin at current dose with cautious monitoring; hold if contrast dye required. Lisinopril and amlodipine continued for hypertension. Repeat creatinine at 4-week follow-up.

8. DIABETES: HbA1c 7.2%, reasonable control. Continue metformin. No change.

9. GENETIC COUNSELING: Previously completed. Relatives (children) advised on BRCA2 germline carrier implications.

10. GOALS OF CARE: Discussed. Patient's goals remain disease control with quality of life preservation. He wishes to remain at home and active as long as possible. Discussed realistic expectations regarding disease trajectory. Hospice was not raised as imminent at this visit, but was acknowledged as a future consideration depending on disease response. Wife present and engaged.

11. RETURN TO CLINIC: 4 weeks for labs and toxicity assessment on olaparib, or sooner PRN for pain escalation or new symptoms.

Time spent with patient and family in discussion of diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment options: approximately 55 minutes.

All 12 biopsy cores are involved by malignancy. Core involvement ranges from 60% to 95% of core length (see table below). The neoplastic glands are arranged in poorly formed, fused, and solid sheet-like patterns without luminal differentiation, consistent with Gleason pattern 5 (primary pattern). Areas exhibiting cribriform architecture with central necrosis (comedonecrosis) are also identified. A secondary component of back-to-back poorly formed and fused glands without well-defined individual glandular lumina is consistent with Gleason pattern 4.

Gleason Score: 5 + 4 = 9 (Grade Group 5)

Summary of core involvement:
  - A1 (Right apex, core 1): 85% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A1 (Right apex, core 2): 90% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A2 (Right mid, core 1): 80% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A2 (Right mid, core 2): 95% involvement, Gleason 5+5=10
  - A3 (Right base, core 1): 75% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A3 (Right base, core 2): 70% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A4 (Left apex, core 1): 85% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A4 (Left apex, core 2): 60% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A5 (Left mid, core 1): 90% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A5 (Left mid, core 2): 75% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A6 (Left base, core 1): 80% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9
  - A6 (Left base, core 2): 65% involvement, Gleason 5+4=9

Total: 12 of 12 cores positive (100%)

PERINEURAL INVASION: Present. Identified in 8 of 12 cores. Extensive perineural invasion noted in right mid and bilateral apex cores, with tumor wrapping circumferentially around large-caliber nerves.

LYMPHOVASCULAR INVASION: Suspicious foci present; lymphovascular spaces adjacent to tumor nests are identified in right mid and left mid cores. Cannot be definitively excluded. Additional tissue level sections reviewed; confirmed as suspicious/present.

INTRADUCTAL CARCINOMA OF THE PROSTATE (IDC-P): Extensive intraductal carcinoma component identified, characterized by expansile cribriform nests filling pre-existing prostatic ductal and acinar spaces with preservation of the basal cell layer (confirmed by p63 and CK5/6 staining; see ancillary studies). IDC-P is present in 7 of 12 cores and comprises a substantial volume of the tumor. This finding is associated with high-grade aggressive pathology and is consistent with the degree of PSA elevation.

NEUROENDOCRINE DIFFERENTIATION: No morphologic evidence of small cell carcinoma or pure neuroendocrine carcinoma. Synaptophysin and chromogranin immunostains are negative (see ancillary studies). No rosette formation, salt-and-pepper chromatin, or scant cytoplasm characteristic of high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma identified.

ADDITIONAL HISTOLOGIC FEATURES:
- High-grade cytologic atypia is present throughout: prominent nucleoli, vesicular chromatin, and moderate to abundant pale cytoplasm.
- Stromal desmoplasia present, focally prominent in right base cores.
- No granulomatous inflammation.
- No evidence of high-grade PIN (masked by invasive carcinoma).
- Benign prostatic tissue present only at the extreme distal ends of the least-involved cores.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed in-house):

1. PSA (polyclonal): POSITIVE — diffuse, strong cytoplasmic and nuclear rim positivity in the majority of neoplastic glands, confirming prostatic origin.

2. PSMA (prostate-specific membrane antigen): POSITIVE — moderate to strong, diffuse membranous positivity in neoplastic cells, supporting prostatic origin.

3. Androgen Receptor (AR): POSITIVE — strong, diffuse nuclear positivity in neoplastic cells (H-score approximately 280/300). Intensity uniform across cores. This finding indicates high AR expression and supports hormone-driven tumor biology.

4. NKX3.1: POSITIVE — strong, diffuse nuclear positivity, corroborating prostatic lineage.

5. ERG (by IHC, clone EPR3864): NEGATIVE — no nuclear ERG expression in neoplastic cells. This result indicates absence of TMPRSS2-ERG gene fusion by IHC surrogate (consistent with negative ERG FISH, if performed).

6. PTEN (by IHC, clone 138G6): LOSS OF EXPRESSION — heterogeneous loss of PTEN protein expression observed. A subset of neoplastic gland-containing areas demonstrates complete absence of PTEN immunostaining while retaining internal stromal control staining, indicative of PTEN deletion/inactivation in the tumor. This finding is heterogeneous, with complete loss in approximately 70% of tumor-containing cores and retained (though reduced) staining in the remainder.

7. Ki-67 (MIB-1): APPROXIMATELY 35% — high proliferative index. Positive nuclei identified in approximately 35% of neoplastic cells on representative hotspot sections (right mid and left apex cores). This is markedly elevated and consistent with high-grade, aggressive biology.

8. p63 / CK5/6 (basal cell cocktail): Used to confirm IDC-P versus invasive carcinoma in selected areas. IDC-P lesions retain a discontinuous peripheral basal cell layer (p63/CK5/6 positive), in contrast to invasive carcinoma (basal cells absent). Both patterns are present as described above.

9. Synaptophysin: NEGATIVE — no neuroendocrine differentiation identified.

10. Chromogranin A: NEGATIVE — no neuroendocrine differentiation identified.

MOLECULAR / GENOMIC STUDIES:
Tissue from this specimen is being submitted to an external reference laboratory for next-generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx, Foundation Medicine). Results PENDING at time of this report. A separate addendum will be issued upon receipt.

Germline genetic testing from peripheral blood has also been ordered and is PENDING; results will be communicated through the Genetics/Genetic Counseling service separately.

FISH STUDIES: TMPRSS2-ERG fusion FISH not performed given negative ERG IHC; clinically not indicated at this time.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
PROSTATE, NEEDLE BIOPSY (12 CORES, TRUS-GUIDED):

ACINAR ADENOCARCINOMA, GRADE GROUP 5
  - Gleason Score: 5 + 4 = 9 (predominant pattern 5 with secondary pattern 4)
  - All 12 of 12 cores positive for carcinoma (100%)
  - Core involvement: 60% to 95% of core length
  - Perineural invasion: PRESENT (8 of 12 cores)
  - Lymphovascular invasion: PRESENT (suspicious/confirmed)
  - Intraductal carcinoma of the prostate (IDC-P): PRESENT, extensive (7 of 12 cores)
  - Neuroendocrine differentiation: NOT IDENTIFIED
  - PTEN expression by IHC: LOSS (heterogeneous)
  - ERG expression by IHC: NEGATIVE
  - AR expression: POSITIVE, strong, diffuse
  - Ki-67 proliferative index: ~35%

  NOTE: Molecular/NGS studies pending; addendum to follow.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
PATHOLOGIC STAGING (biopsy specimen only):
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
Pathologic T stage cannot be assigned on needle biopsy alone. Clinical staging per MRI and clinical examination: cT3b (bilateral seminal vesicle invasion), N1, M1b per treating team.

Findings are consistent with and support the clinical diagnosis of high-volume, high-grade, clinically metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
This is a morphologically high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma with multiple adverse pathologic features: Grade Group 5 (Gleason 9), 100% core positivity, extensive perineural invasion, suspicious lymphovascular invasion, and prominent intraductal carcinoma. The absence of neuroendocrine differentiation on the current specimen is noted; however, the high Ki-67 index (~35%) and extensive IDC-P component warrant close clinical follow-up for potential treatment-emergent neuroendocrine transformation if the patient is placed on prolonged androgen deprivation therapy. The PTEN loss by IHC is consistent with a clinically aggressive phenotype and may have implications for PI3K pathway-directed therapy in the future. ERG negativity by IHC is noted.

All immunohistochemical stains were performed with appropriate positive and negative controls.

MULTIPARAMETRIC MRI OF THE LIVER WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST
AND
18F-FDG PET/CT WHOLE BODY

CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), germline BRCA2 mutation, currently on olaparib (dose-reduced to 100 mg BID). Recent restaging CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated two new hypodense hepatic lesions in the right lobe (1.1 cm and 0.9 cm) and two small indeterminate pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe. Studies obtained for further lesion characterization and comprehensive disease reassessment.

---

MRI LIVER WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM (MULTIPHASE)

TECHNIQUE: Multiparametric MRI of the liver performed on a 3.0 Tesla system. Sequences obtained include axial and coronal T2-weighted fat-saturated images, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping (b-values 0, 400, 800 s/mm2), pre-contrast T1-weighted in-phase and opposed-phase gradient echo imaging, and dynamic gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted sequences obtained in the late arterial, portal venous, and 5-minute delayed phases following intravenous administration of gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadobenate dimeglumine, MultiHance, 0.1 mmol/kg). Hepatobiliary phase imaging obtained at 20 minutes. Respiratory triggering and breath-hold techniques employed throughout.

FINDINGS:

Liver: The liver is mildly enlarged with a craniocaudal span of 18.4 cm. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates mildly heterogeneous signal on T2-weighted sequences, likely reflecting background changes related to known systemic disease burden. No evidence of hepatic steatosis on in-phase/opposed-phase imaging. No hepatic iron overload.

Two focal lesions are identified in segments VI-VII of the right hepatic lobe:

Lesion 1 (dominant lesion, segment VII): Measures 1.2 cm in greatest axial dimension on T1 post-contrast imaging (previously 1.1 cm on CT). This lesion demonstrates low T1 signal on pre-contrast imaging and mildly elevated T2 signal relative to the background hepatic parenchyma. On DWI, there is restricted diffusion with corresponding low ADC value (approximately 0.82 x 10^-3 mm2/s), consistent with cellular malignancy. Following gadolinium administration, the lesion demonstrates heterogeneous arterial phase enhancement (arterial phase signal intensity ratio lesion:liver approximately 1.6:1), with progressive washout on the portal venous and delayed phases, yielding a relative signal loss compared to enhancing liver parenchyma on 5-minute delayed imaging. On hepatobiliary phase imaging at 20 minutes, the lesion is hypointense relative to the liver, indicating absence of functional hepatocytes and further supporting a malignant diagnosis. No central necrosis. No hemorrhage.

Lesion 2 (segment VI): Measures 1.0 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 0.9 cm on CT). Demonstrates identical imaging characteristics to lesion 1 with T2 hyperintensity, restricted diffusion (ADC approximately 0.89 x 10^-3 mm2/s), arterial phase enhancement with delayed washout, and hepatobiliary phase hypointensity. This lesion abuts but does not appear to involve the adjacent hepatic venous branch.

No additional hepatic lesions are definitively identified above the limit of detection at this field strength.

Biliary System: The intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary ducts are not dilated. The common bile duct measures 4 mm in caliber. No choledocholithiasis or biliary mass.

Gallbladder: Gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. No wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid.

Portal and Hepatic Veins: Portal vein is patent with normal hepatopetal flow on dynamic enhancement. Hepatic veins are patent. No hepatic vein or portal vein thrombosis.

Pancreas: Not fully evaluated on this liver-protocol study; however, visualized portions of the pancreatic head and body appear unremarkable without focal mass or ductal dilation.

Spleen: The spleen measures 13.8 cm in length, mildly enlarged. No focal splenic lesion.

IMPRESSION (MRI LIVER):
1. Two lesions in hepatic segments VI-VII demonstrating arterial phase enhancement, delayed washout, restricted diffusion, and hepatobiliary phase hypointensity. In the setting of known metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with recent biochemical and radiographic progression, these features are most consistent with hepatic metastases. The washout kinetics in these lesions, while somewhat atypical for hypervascular metastases (which are less commonly seen with prostate primaries), likely reflect the high-grade biology and possible early neuroendocrine features of this patient's disease. Tissue sampling is strongly warranted for confirmation and biomarker assessment.
2. Mild splenomegaly, possibly related to systemic disease burden or portal hypertension from hepatic infiltration, though the degree is mild.
3. No biliary obstruction.

---

18F-FDG PET/CT, WHOLE BODY

TECHNIQUE: Whole-body FDG PET/CT performed from vertex to mid-thighs following intravenous administration of 10.2 mCi (377 MBq) of fluorine-18 fluorodeoxyglucose. Patient fasted for 6 hours prior to injection. Blood glucose at time of injection: 118 mg/dL. Images acquired 65 minutes post-injection. Attenuation correction performed using the concurrent low-dose CT. CT technique: helical acquisition, 120 kVp, automated mA modulation, 5 mm reconstructed slice thickness. PET images reconstructed using iterative algorithm with time-of-flight correction.

FINDINGS:

Head and Neck: Brain demonstrates normal physiologic FDG distribution with no focal areas of abnormal uptake or photopenic defect to suggest metastatic involvement. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No FDG-avid lesions in the head or neck.

Chest:
Lungs: Two small nodules are identified in the right lower lobe on the low-dose CT component. The larger nodule measures 5 mm in the right lower lobe lateral basal segment and demonstrates very low-level FDG uptake with an SUVmax of approximately 1.8 (mildly above background mediastinal blood pool SUVmax 1.5). The second right lower lobe nodule measures 4 mm with negligible FDG uptake (SUVmax 1.2), statistically indistinguishable from background. No left-sided pulmonary nodules. No FDG-avid pulmonary masses. The low-level avidity in the larger right lower lobe nodule is of indeterminate significance; subcentimeter nodules are frequently below the spatial resolution of PET for reliable metabolic characterization. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy on CT. No FDG-avid mediastinal nodes.

Cardiovascular: Cardiac chambers demonstrate physiologic FDG distribution. No pericardial effusion.

Abdomen and Pelvis:
Liver: Two foci of moderately increased FDG uptake are identified in the right hepatic lobe corresponding to the lesions characterized on the concurrent MRI. The dominant lesion in segment VII demonstrates an SUVmax of 5.8. The second lesion in segment VI demonstrates an SUVmax of 4.2. Both lesions show focal, well-defined metabolic activity clearly above background hepatic parenchymal uptake (mean hepatic SUV 2.3). These foci are spatially concordant with the MRI lesions and with the CT hypodense foci identified on the prior staging CT. The degree of FDG avidity, while moderate rather than intensely hypermetabolic, is consistent with metastatic deposits and is notable given that prostate adenocarcinoma classically demonstrates variable and sometimes low FDG uptake; the moderate avidity seen here likely reflects the high-grade, treatment-emergent biology of this lesion.

Splenic, pancreatic, adrenal, renal, and gastrointestinal FDG uptake: within normal physiologic limits. No FDG-avid abdominal visceral lesions beyond the hepatic foci described. No ascites.

Lymph Nodes: Multiple mildly to moderately FDG-avid lymph nodes are identified in the pelvis and retroperitoneum:
- Right obturator lymph node: 1.6 cm on CT (stable compared to most recent CT), SUVmax 3.4, mildly avid.
- Left common iliac node: 0.8 cm, SUVmax 2.6, mildly avid.
- Right para-aortic node: 1.4 cm, SUVmax 3.1, mildly avid.
- Aortocaval node: 1.1 cm, SUVmax 2.8, mildly avid.
Appearance is consistent with nodal metastatic disease, stable compared to prior imaging. No new FDG-avid nodal stations identified.

Osseous Structures: The whole-body PET/CT identifies extensive, multifocal FDG-avid osseous disease at numerous sites throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton. Sites of increased FDG uptake include:
- L2 vertebral body: SUVmax 4.1
- T9 vertebral body: SUVmax 3.8
- T12 vertebral body: SUVmax 3.6
- Sacrum (right lateral mass): SUVmax 3.9
- Left iliac crest (two adjacent foci): SUVmax 4.4 and 3.7
- Left iliac wing: SUVmax 3.2
- Right acetabulum: SUVmax 3.4
- Right femoral neck and proximal femur: SUVmax 2.6 (reduced compared to expected; patient has prior radiation to this site, and photopenic appearance on one side is consistent with post-RT changes)
- Left femoral head: SUVmax 3.1
- Right proximal humerus: SUVmax 4.0
- Right 4th rib: SUVmax 3.2
- Left 6th and 7th ribs: SUVmax 3.0 and 2.9
Overall osseous disease burden is extensive but without clearly new hypermetabolic foci compared to the most recently available bone scan reference. No evidence of impending pathologic fracture at weight-bearing sites on CT, though cortical thinning is noted at the right femoral neck consistent with known treatment history.

Prostate/Pelvis: The prostate bed demonstrates no definite FDG-avid mass. Post-treatment changes noted.

IMPRESSION (PET/CT):
1. Two foci of moderately increased FDG uptake in the right hepatic lobe (segment VI and VII, SUVmax 5.8 and 4.2) are spatially concordant with the hepatic lesions characterized by MRI and prior CT. The combined imaging features across MRI and PET/CT are most consistent with hepatic metastases from the patient's known high-grade castration-resistant prostate cancer. The degree of FDG avidity is above what would typically be expected for low-grade prostate adenocarcinoma and may reflect the evolving high-grade/neuroendocrine biology documented by this patient's clinical course. Tissue confirmation is strongly warranted.
2. Two small right lower lobe pulmonary nodules (5 mm and 4 mm) with low or negligible FDG uptake. These remain indeterminate; the low metabolic activity may reflect small size and partial-volume averaging limitations. Cannot exclude early pulmonary metastatic deposits given the degree of overall disease burden. Close interval follow-up or tissue sampling as clinically appropriate.
3. Stable, mildly-to-moderately FDG-avid pelvic and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy involving right obturator, left common iliac, right para-aortic, and aortocaval nodal stations, consistent with known nodal metastatic disease, without clear progression compared to prior imaging.
4. Extensive hypermetabolic multifocal osseous metastatic disease involving the axial and appendicular skeleton at the numerous sites described above. No convincingly new osseous lesions compared to the prior bone scan, with the caveat that FDG PET/CT and conventional bone scintigraphy use different detection mechanisms (metabolic vs. osteoblastic) and direct comparison has inherent limitations.
5. No FDG-avid intracranial lesions. No FDG-avid mediastinal or supraclavicular disease.

OVERALL IMPRESSION: New hepatic metastases confirmed by combined gadolinium-enhanced MRI and FDG PET/CT. The imaging features on both modalities are concordant and consistent with metastatic disease from the patient's known castration-resistant prostate carcinoma, with the moderate FDG avidity raising the possibility of evolving high-grade/neuroendocrine histology. Indeterminate small pulmonary nodules without convincing hypermetabolism, significance to be determined in clinical context. Stable nodal and osseous metastatic disease overall.

GADOLINIUM-ENHANCED MRI OF THE LIVER AND WHOLE-BODY FDG PET/CT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with known metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9, with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant. He has been on olaparib (currently at reduced dose 100 mg BID following dose reductions for hematologic toxicity) for approximately 7 months, having previously progressed through ADT plus abiraterone and enzalutamide. Recent restaging CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated two new hypodense hepatic lesions in the right hepatic lobe (1.1 cm and 0.9 cm) and two small new right lower lobe pulmonary nodules (5 mm and 4 mm). This combined MRI liver and PET/CT was obtained for further lesion characterization and to evaluate for additional sites of disease involvement. Background of extensive osseous metastatic disease and pelvic/retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

------------------------------------------------------------
EXAM 1: MRI LIVER WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM
------------------------------------------------------------

TECHNIQUE:
Multiparametric MRI of the abdomen with dedicated hepatic protocol performed on a 3.0 Tesla MRI system. Sequences obtained: axial T1 in-phase and out-of-phase, axial and coronal T2 fat-saturated, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI b=50, 400, 800) with corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) maps, and dynamic gadolinium-enhanced sequences including arterial, portal venous, and delayed hepatobiliary phase imaging following administration of 15 mL of gadobutrol (Gadavist) intravenously. Image quality: good to excellent, minor motion artifact on arterial phase sequences only, not limiting.

FINDINGS:

Liver:
The liver is normal in overall size and contour. No gross hepatic steatosis. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous signal on non-enhanced sequences.

Two focal lesions are identified in the posterior right hepatic lobe:

Lesion 1 (segment VI/VII): Measuring 1.2 cm in greatest dimension (previously 1.1 cm on CT). On T2-weighted imaging this lesion demonstrates mildly increased signal relative to hepatic parenchyma. On diffusion-weighted imaging there is restricted diffusion with low ADC values (ADC approximately 0.78 x 10^-3 mm2/s), indicating high cellularity. On dynamic gadolinium-enhanced series, this lesion demonstrates avid arterial phase enhancement becoming mildly hypointense on portal venous and delayed phase imaging relative to surrounding liver (washout appearance). The lesion appears well-circumscribed without internal hemorrhage or necrosis.

Lesion 2 (segment VI): Measuring 1.0 cm in greatest dimension (previously 0.9 cm on CT). Similar signal characteristics to lesion 1: mildly T2 hyperintense, restricted diffusion on DWI (ADC approximately 0.81 x 10^-3 mm2/s), arterial phase enhancement with washout on delayed phase. Well-circumscribed.

The enhancement pattern and diffusion characteristics of both lesions are not consistent with benign entities such as simple cysts, hemangioma, or focal nodular hyperplasia. The combination of restricted diffusion and washout enhancement in the setting of known disseminated prostate cancer is most consistent with hepatic metastatic deposits.

No additional hepatic lesions are identified. No satellite nodules.

Biliary system: The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are within normal limits. No biliary ductal dilation. Gallbladder unremarkable, no gallstones, no pericholecystic fluid.

Portal vein: Patent with normal hepatopetal flow characteristics.

Hepatic veins: All three hepatic veins visualized and patent.

Spleen: Normal in size (10.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension) without focal lesions.

Pancreas: No focal lesions. Pancreatic duct not dilated. Peripancreatic fat plane intact.

Kidneys: Bilateral simple renal cysts, stable. No hydronephrosis. The left kidney measures 10.4 cm; the right kidney 10.1 cm. Renal cortical thinning bilaterally, consistent with chronic parenchymal changes in the setting of known CKD.

Adrenal glands: No adrenal lesions or enlargement identified.

Lymph nodes: Several enlarged lymph nodes in the portocaval and hepatic hilar regions, the largest measuring approximately 1.0 cm in short axis in the portocaval space. These are not definitively enlarged by size criteria but are noted given the clinical context. Pelvic and retroperitoneal adenopathy not fully assessed on this limited abdominal protocol.

Ascites: None.

------------------------------------------------------------
EXAM 2: WHOLE-BODY FDG PET/CT
------------------------------------------------------------

TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body F-18 fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) PET/CT performed from vertex to mid-thigh following a 60-minute uptake period after intravenous injection of 10.8 mCi F-18 FDG. Serum glucose at time of injection: 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated; patient is a known type 2 diabetic on metformin, which was held appropriately for 48 hours prior). Attenuation correction performed using low-dose CT component. Images reconstructed using time-of-flight reconstruction. Comparison made to prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis.

FINDINGS:

Head and Neck:
No abnormal FDG-avid foci in the head or neck. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy with pathologic FDG uptake.

Chest:
Lungs: Two small nodules in the right lower lobe as previously identified on CT are re-demonstrated. The larger nodule, now measuring approximately 5 mm, demonstrates mildly above-background FDG activity with an SUVmax of 1.8. The smaller 4 mm nodule demonstrates similarly low-level FDG activity, SUVmax 1.6. The degree of FDG avidity is below thresholds typically associated with confidently metabolically active malignancy at these sizes, though the combination of new appearance on interval CT and any above-background uptake in this clinical context warrants ongoing close attention. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy with FDG activity above mediastinal blood pool. No pericardial effusion.

Bones of the thorax: Multifocal areas of moderately-to-markedly increased FDG uptake throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton consistent with the patient's known extensive osseous metastatic disease. Notable hypermetabolic foci in the thorax include:
- T9 vertebral body: moderate FDG uptake, SUVmax 4.1 (known lesion, stable compared to clinical history)
- T12 vertebral body: mild residual FDG uptake, SUVmax 2.9 (known lesion, appears reduced in activity compared to prior progression)
- Multiple rib lesions, bilateral, with heterogeneous FDG avidity (SUVmax range 2.1 to 4.3); no new definitive rib lesions identified
- Right proximal humerus: mildly increased FDG uptake, SUVmax 3.2, corresponding to a sclerotic lesion on CT

Abdomen:
Liver: The two hepatic lesions identified on the liver MRI (segments VI and VII) demonstrate focal FDG uptake clearly above hepatic background:
- Segment VI/VII lesion (1.2 cm): SUVmax 5.8. This level of metabolic activity is clearly pathologic.
- Segment VI lesion (1.0 cm): SUVmax 4.2. Similarly metabolically active.
The FDG avidity of these hepatic lesions, in conjunction with the MRI enhancement characteristics (arterial enhancement with washout, restricted diffusion), is convincingly consistent with viable hepatic metastases. No additional FDG-avid hepatic lesions are identified. The SUVmax values are moderate; this level of FDG avidity is not atypical for prostate cancer visceral metastases, which are frequently only moderately FDG-avid relative to primary tumors with neuroendocrine features.

Lymph nodes:
- Right obturator lymph node: mildly increased FDG uptake, SUVmax 3.1, corresponding to the known enlarged node (approximately 1.6 cm on CT, previously 2.1 cm on prior CT at time of enzalutamide progression).
- Left common iliac node: low-level FDG activity, SUVmax 2.2.
- Right para-aortic node: mildly FDG avid, SUVmax 2.7. Aortocaval node: low-level activity, SUVmax 1.9.
- Portocaval region nodes: mild FDG uptake corresponding to the borderline-size nodes noted on MRI, SUVmax 2.4. These may represent reactive or early involved nodes; given the adjacent hepatic metastases, cannot exclude early nodal spread to the hepatic hilum.

Spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, kidneys: No pathologic FDG uptake. No FDG-avid lesions.

Pelvis:
- Prostate/seminal vesicle bed: mild diffuse low-level FDG uptake without discrete focus; likely post-treatment change.
- Pelvic osseous lesions: continued FDG avidity in the left iliac crest (two foci, SUVmax 4.6 and 3.8), sacrum (SUVmax 3.9), right acetabulum (SUVmax 3.7). Left iliac wing: persistent but mildly decreased FDG uptake compared to prior clinical description (SUVmax 3.1).

Lower Extremities:
- Right femoral neck and proximal femur: markedly decreased FDG uptake in the region of prior radiation (30 Gy in 10 fractions), now SUVmax 1.4, consistent with treatment effect from palliative radiation therapy.
- Left femoral head: residual mild FDG uptake, SUVmax 2.8, consistent with known osseous metastasis.

Brain (vertex):
Limited evaluation on this protocol; no obviously FDG-avid lesions in the vertex region included in the scan field.

------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION
------------------------------------------------------------

1. HEPATIC METASTASES CONFIRMED: The two right hepatic lobe lesions (segment VI/VII, 1.2 cm, and segment VI, 1.0 cm) demonstrate combined MRI features of arterial enhancement with delayed washout and restricted diffusion, together with moderate-to-marked FDG avidity on PET (SUVmax 5.8 and 4.2). These findings are diagnostic of hepatic metastatic disease in this clinical context. Biopsy correlation may be considered if tissue confirmation would impact clinical management.

2. INDETERMINATE PULMONARY NODULES, LOW-LEVEL FDG AVIDITY: Two right lower lobe pulmonary nodules (5 mm and 4 mm) demonstrate only low-level FDG uptake (SUVmax 1.8 and 1.6), below the threshold for confident characterization as metabolically active metastases. However, given their new appearance on interval CT and the patient's known disseminated malignancy, these nodules remain indeterminate and cannot be excluded as early pulmonary metastatic deposits. Dedicated CT chest with thin-section reconstruction at the time of next restaging is recommended for interval size assessment.

3. EXTENSIVE OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Multifocal hypermetabolic osseous involvement throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton consistent with known widely disseminated bone metastases. Overall pattern appears stable-to-mildly improved compared to clinical reference point of enzalutamide progression, with continued treatment effect from prior palliative radiation to the right femoral neck (markedly reduced FDG uptake at that site). No new osseous lesions definitively identified on this study.

4. LYMPHADENOPATHY, STABLE: Pelvic and retroperitoneal lymph nodes demonstrate mild FDG avidity consistent with known nodal metastatic disease. The right obturator node (1.6 cm) appears slightly reduced in size compared to prior progression imaging and the FDG activity is moderate. Borderline portocaval and hepatic hilar nodes with low-level FDG uptake are noted in the vicinity of the hepatic metastases; involvement cannot be excluded.

5. NO ADDITIONAL VISCERAL SITES: No FDG-avid lesions identified in the adrenal glands, spleen, pancreas, or bone marrow on this survey that would suggest additional previously unrecognized sites of spread beyond what has been described.

  Core B: Measures 1.5 cm in length x 0.15 cm in diameter. The tissue is similarly pale tan, with focal areas of slightly firmer, whitish tissue suggestive of infiltrative tumor. Submitted entirely in one cassette (B1).

No normal hepatic parenchyma with recognizable trabecular architecture is grossly appreciated in either core. No bile, necrotic material, or calcification noted grossly. Representative sections entirely submitted.

Cassettes: A1 (core A, entirely submitted), B1 (core B, entirely submitted). Total cassettes: 2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

Sections from both cores (A1 and B1) show hypercellular tissue composed of infiltrating epithelial neoplasm. The background hepatic parenchyma is largely replaced or compressed by tumor, with rare residual hepatocytes seen at the periphery of core B showing mild steatosis and non-specific reactive changes.

The neoplasm is composed predominantly of poorly differentiated glandular structures and solid nests/sheets of epithelial cells. Individual cells demonstrate enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei with prominent nucleoli, scant to moderate amphophilic cytoplasm, and an increased nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio. Cribriform and fused glandular architecture is focally present, consistent with Gleason pattern 5 morphology in the context of prostate primary. True acinar formation, where identifiable, is markedly distorted. Scattered individual tumor cells and small cell clusters are present in a desmoplastic stromal background.

High-grade cytologic features are prominent: mitotic figures are readily identified (approximately 8-10 per 10 high power fields), including atypical forms. Apoptotic bodies are scattered throughout the tumor.

FOCAL FINDING: A minor component (estimated <5% of total tumor cells in the submitted cores) demonstrates cells with more plasmacytoid, rounded nuclei, finely granular chromatin, indistinct nucleoli, and scant cytoplasm, arranged in loose nests without glandular formation. This morphology raises the possibility of focal neuroendocrine differentiation, which is further evaluated by ancillary studies (see below).

No lymphovascular invasion is identified in the limited biopsy material. No perineural invasion is identified. There is no lymphoid aggregate or granulomatous inflammation to suggest an infectious etiology. No hemorrhagic necrosis or geographic necrosis identified within this limited sampling.

Residue liver parenchyma (core B, peripheral): residual hepatocytes show compressed, atrophied trabeculae with minimal reactive fibrosis at the tumor-liver interface. No cirrhotic nodules, no bile duct obstruction. Portal tracts show mild chronic inflammatory infiltrate.

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS

SPECIMEN A (Core A) AND SPECIMEN B (Core B):

METASTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH PROSTATIC ORIGIN.

  - Morphology and immunophenotype are consistent with metastatic prostatic acinar adenocarcinoma with high-grade (Gleason pattern 5-equivalent) features in a metastatic site.
  - Strong and diffuse NKX3.1 and PSMA positivity, in conjunction with clinical history of Gleason 9 prostate carcinoma, confirms prostatic primary.
  - Focal synaptophysin positivity (<5% of cells) in the context of absent chromogranin, CD56, and INSM1, combined with retained prostatic lineage marker expression (NKX3.1, PSMA), is most consistent with focal neuroendocrine differentiation (microacinar neuroendocrine features) rather than overt small cell/neuroendocrine carcinoma.
  - The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings, in the setting of RB1 loss, TP53 mutation (per prior genomic testing), and high Ki-67 (~55-60%), are consistent with early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC) features as a minor component, superimposed on a dominant adenocarcinoma morphology. The diagnosis of frank small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma is NOT supported by the current specimen.
  - Markedly elevated Ki-67 (~55-60%) compared to primary biopsy (~35%) indicates significant increase in proliferative activity, consistent with disease evolution under treatment selective pressure.
  - Loss of PTEN and RB1 protein expression by IHC is concordant with prior genomic data.
  - AR heterogeneity (moderate, ~60-70% of cells) may reflect clonal evolution and AR-dependent and AR-independent tumor subpopulations.

Note: Correlation with AR FISH (pending) and repeat comprehensive genomic profiling (submitted separately, pending) is strongly recommended for complete molecular characterization of metastatic disease and to identify potential resistance mechanisms and therapeutic targets.
